caveats: a: The occupation data cover NAICS 332 rather than 332510 or the outsourced subset.
a: ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
a: Existing CNC, stamping, and conventional automation are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
rho: No source measures AI implementation in eligible hardware contract manufacturers.
rho: Small plants with paper travelers and disconnected machines may implement much more slowly.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the outsourced-service lens.
e: Private-label sales do not always constitute customer-controlled outsourced manufacturing.
s5: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
s5: Age, intent to sell, and a completed qualifying transfer are different quantities.
q: No public contract sample establishes pass-through behavior for the eligible population.
q: Commodity hardware and concentrated customers can make retention materially lower.
d5: Industrial production includes proprietary and commodity hardware outside the lens.
d5: Nominal construction spending is not constant-price demand and covers many inputs besides hardware.
d5: Product mix can diverge materially between residential, commercial, institutional, and OEM hardware.
o: Operator requirement can persist while work shifts offshore or to a larger supplier.
o: Demand elimination and import substitution are reflected primarily in d5, not this value.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy recurring production runs under drawings, tooling, finish, quality, packaging, and delivery specifications, typically through purchase orders, blanket agreements, or annual supply contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes both proprietary standardized products and customer-specific contract production. The lens retains recurring outsourced manufacturing so eligibility and value capture describe one coherent model.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers selling only their own standardized branded hardware, captive plants, pure distributors, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly produce customer-designed or private-label hinges, handles, keys, locks, and related metal hardware for external OEM, distributor, or building-products customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all fabricated-metal manufacturing rather than eligible hardware contract manufacturers, while ILO reports global GenAI exposure by broad occupation. I map higher exposure to quoting, engineering documentation, purchasing, scheduling, quality records, customer service, and administration and lower exposure to machining, pressing, assembly, finishing, inspection execution, and material handling. The broad BLS mix may overstate welding and machining relative to hardware plants but still demonstrates a production-heavy workforce; GenAI-only evidence may understate machine-vision opportunity.
caveats: The occupation data cover NAICS 332 rather than 332510 or the outsourced subset.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
Existing CNC, stamping, and conventional automation are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from at least ten eligible hardware suppliers, separating already automated tasks from current manual time across quoting, engineering, production, quality, and administration, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: BLS reports large fabricated-metal employment in machinists, assemblers, welders, press operators, and production supervisors. AI can assist information workflows and some inspection or maintenance analysis, but most direct fabrication and assembly tasks require equipment or robotics and accountable physical execution.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production measures all U.S. hardware manufacturing, not five-year constant-price demand for outsourced lower-middle-market suppliers, while Census construction spending is a broad downstream indicator. The hardware index fell from 102.3244 in 2019 Q4 to 78.7915 in 2025 Q4, and May 2026 construction spending was 1.5% below a year earlier. I translate those weak current signals into modest base contraction with a wide recovery range. The broad hardware index may overstate weakness for specialized qualified components or understate import pressure on commodity products.
caveats: Industrial production includes proprietary and commodity hardware outside the lens.
Nominal construction spending is not constant-price demand and covers many inputs besides hardware.
Product mix can diverge materially between residential, commercial, institutional, and OEM hardware.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-specific forecasts, bookings, backlog, import penetration, housing and renovation indicators, and product-level shipment histories from eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Hardware is a recurring physical input to construction, repair, furniture, vehicles, equipment, and access systems, but domestic production faces imports, housing and construction cycles, product standardization, and customer inventory swings. Recent production and construction evidence argues against assuming near-term growth.

caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the outsourced-service lens.
Private-label sales do not always constitute customer-controlled outsourced manufacturing.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 156 dataset firms using customer drawings, ownership of tooling and intellectual property, private-label revenue, repeat contracts, captive status, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines the code around metal hardware products rather than a service model. Private-label and customer-drawing production can qualify, but many firms primarily own designs, brands, catalogs, or distribution relationships and therefore sell products rather than outsourced manufacturing.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $2,279.8M / sectoral output (T30) $9,178.1M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $371,385 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332510); class <5: 165 firms, avg revenue/firm $591,806 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $116,112 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,656,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $324,956 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 78 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,162,808 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $620,543 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,970,676 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,348,647 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,598,292 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,927,385 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $205,234,086 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,266,928 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 156

caveats: Operator requirement can persist while work shifts offshore or to a larger supplier.
Demand elimination and import substitution are reflected primarily in d5, not this value.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, supplier-consolidation data, additive-manufacturing qualification studies, and evidence of fully autonomous approved production would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical cutting, forming, machining, finishing, assembly, testing, and delivery by an accountable manufacturer. Software can automate information work but cannot provide the metal part; displacement is mainly customer insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, additive manufacture, or product redesign.

caveats: No public contract sample establishes pass-through behavior for the eligible population.
Commodity hardware and concentrated customers can make retention materially lower.
q.change_evidence: A contract and purchase-order sample showing term, repricing cadence, metal pass-through, open-book provisions, productivity clauses, tooling ownership, and historical price concessions would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Tooling, qualification, service levels, and switching cost can preserve savings during a program, especially when benefits improve delivery or quality. Annual bids, should-cost analysis, metal surcharges, open-book costing, and strong OEM or distributor procurement transmit visible productivity gains to customers.

caveats: No source measures AI implementation in eligible hardware contract manufacturers.
Small plants with paper travelers and disconnected machines may implement much more slowly.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring accepted AI outputs, labor hours, quote cycle time, schedule adherence, scrap, false inspection calls, and integration cost across representative hardware workflows would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Quoting, scheduling, procurement, document control, and quality analytics can be implemented within five years. Legacy ERP and machine data, drawing variation, cybersecurity, customer approvals, workforce adoption, and the need to validate safety and dimensional accuracy limit implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not qualifying hardware-company control transfers. I use this only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, internal succession, non-sale exits, multi-owner firms, customer concentration, obsolete equipment, and operations that are not transferable. The cross-industry proxy may overstate sale probability for capital-intensive plants but may understate succession pressure in founder-led shops.
caveats: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age, intent to sell, and a completed qualifying transfer are different quantities.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from industrial M&A databases, UCC and ownership records, and interviews with hardware-sector brokers, owners, and lenders covering the prior five years would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Founder succession can create seller supply, but completed control transfers require a durable customer book, usable tooling and equipment, documented processes, environmental compliance, and independence from the exiting owner.

principal_driver: Recurring customer drawings and qualification create sticky programs with improvable quoting, planning, and quality workflows.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and domestic hardware production has shown sustained weakness, limiting both automation scope and demand confidence.

sources.supports: Census defines 332510 as manufacturing metal hardware such as hinges, handles, keys, and locks other than coin-operated and time locks.
sources.title: NAICS Definition: Hardware Manufacturing

sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 fabricated-metal employment of 56,060 press operators, 67,090 production supervisors, 101,640 machinists, 125,250 team assemblers, and 112,520 welders; it also reports 2024 output fell 4.0%.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: ILO reports 24% of clerical tasks at high automation exposure and 58% at medium exposure, while other occupational groups have only 1% to 4% highly exposed and no more than 25% medium-exposed tasks.
sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: Policies to Manage the Transition

sources.supports: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: The Federal Reserve industrial-production series reported a hardware index of 102.3244 in 2019 Q4 and 78.7915 in 2025 Q4, with 2017 equal to 100.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Hardware Manufacturing

sources.supports: Census reports May 2026 construction spending at a $2,210.2 billion annual rate, 1.5% below May 2025, with private residential spending at $930.2 billion and private nonresidential spending at $738.7 billion.
sources.title: Monthly Construction Spending, May 2026

title: Hardware Manufacturing
